rationale: Gunasti and Ross (2010) is used beyond a passing mention: it is cited as core prior work on alphanumeric brand names in the literature positioning and again in the discussion to benchmark effect sizes. The citing paper explicitly contrasts its field-based demand estimates and real sales outcomes with prior lab-based choice effects, using Gunasti and Ross as a concrete reference point. This role materially informs the paper's framing of contribution and interpretation of findings.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Gunasti and Ross (2010) by moving from experimental evidence on alphanumeric name effects to observational, market-level demand estimation in China's auto market across multiple brand-name types. It also uses the earlier study's larger lab effect size as a benchmark to argue that field effects are smaller but behaviorally meaningful.
